skill.name: runcomfy-cli
skill.description: Use the RunComfy CLI to authenticate, inspect model schemas, submit media generation jobs, poll status, and download results.
skill.summary: Run RunComfy model endpoints from a terminal through an authenticated CLI workflow.
skill.author: agentspace-so

security_audit.summary: The static findings are mostly true detections of Markdown examples, documented URLs, and intended RunComfy CLI use, not evidence of malware. The skill still carries medium operational risk because it guides an authenticated external CLI, sends prompts and asset references to RunComfy, stores a local token, and downloads generated files.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated External CLI Operations
medium_findings.description: The skill intentionally enables Bash access to runcomfy commands and documents authenticated model submission, polling, cancellation, and downloads. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but it sends user prompts and job data to RunComfy services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration and workflow text directly show intended external CLI and network use. The context is legitimate service operation, not hidden exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Credential Storage and Environment Token Use
medium_findings.description: The skill documents storing a RunComfy token in the user configuration directory and optionally using RUNCOMFY_TOKEN. This is expected authentication behavior, but users must avoid exposing tokens in prompts, logs, or repositories.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The token file path and environment token option are explicitly documented. The text also includes mitigation guidance, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Generated Output Downloads to Local Filesystem
medium_findings.description: The CLI downloads generated output files to the current directory or a user selected output directory. This can consume disk space or write files into unintended locations if paths are chosen carelessly.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill clearly describes download behavior and a file size cap. The behavior is expected, but it affects the local filesystem.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Triggered Static Shell Rules
low_findings.description: Many external command detections are fenced documentation examples for installing or using the RunComfy CLI. They are visible user-facing instructions rather than hidden executable code inside the skill package.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matched content is in Markdown code fences and prose. No separate script file or hidden execution hook was found.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Product Documentation and Service Endpoints
low_findings.description: The URL detections point to RunComfy documentation, model catalog pages, generated output examples, and documented RunComfy API or download domains. No unrelated callback or suspicious third-party endpoint was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The domains are related to the advertised RunComfy service and documentation. The skill also states a narrow endpoint allowlist.

low_findings.title: High-Severity Static Filesystem and Crypto Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The path traversal match is an ellipsis in an example output URL, and the weak cryptography matches do not correspond to cryptographic code. Hidden file detections refer to normal token storage documentation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line context shows documentation examples, not executable traversal or cryptographic implementation. Token storage remains covered as a separate medium operational risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Invocation Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill's core workflow depends on running runcomfy commands from Bash. This pattern is expected but requires user authorization and careful handling of arguments.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicitly declared and repeatedly documented. It is dangerous in capability, but aligned with the skill purpose.

dangerous_patterns.title: Outbound Network Submission and Download Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents posting model requests, polling status, and downloading generated outputs from RunComfy domains. Users should treat submitted prompts and assets as data shared with an external service.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The network behavior is described directly in the skill. The listed endpoints match the advertised service rather than hidden destinations.

content.user_title: Run RunComfy Models from the CLI
content.value_statement: Media model workflows often require manual setup, schema lookup, and polling. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical RunComfy CLI guidance for repeatable terminal automation.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to install and verify the RunComfy CLI.
Guides browser and token based RunComfy authentication.
Shows how to submit model runs with inline or file based inputs.
Covers polling, no-wait jobs, status checks, retries, and exit codes.
Documents output download options and machine-readable output mode.
Points users to RunComfy model catalog and API schema pages.
limitations: Requires the RunComfy CLI to be installed before use.
Requires a valid RunComfy account and token for authenticated runs.
Does not include model-specific schemas inside the skill.
Generated media quality, cost, speed, and availability depend on RunComfy services.

use_cases.title: Automate creative asset generation
use_cases.description: Submit image or video generation jobs from a repeatable terminal workflow.

use_cases.title: Build media processing scripts
use_cases.description: Use JSON output, request IDs, retries, and output directories in shell automation.

use_cases.title: Run model experiments
use_cases.description: Discover RunComfy model schemas and compare endpoints with controlled inputs.

prompt_templates.title: Check CLI setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the runcomfy-cli skill to help me verify that RunComfy CLI is installed and that I am signed in.

prompt_templates.title: Run one model
prompt_templates.prompt: Use RunComfy CLI to prepare a single text-to-image run and explain where the output will be saved.

prompt_templates.title: Submit and poll later
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to submit a RunComfy job without waiting, save the request ID, and check the status later.

prompt_templates.title: Create batch workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a RunComfy CLI batch workflow that reads prompts from a file, writes each result to a separate folder, and handles retryable failures.

output_examples.input: Ask for help generating one image through RunComfy CLI.
output_examples.output: The assistant identifies the model endpoint, confirms authentication, prepares the request, and explains the expected downloaded image location.

output_examples.input: Ask for a no-wait video generation workflow.
output_examples.output: The assistant describes submission, request ID capture, later status checks, cancellation behavior, and download handling.

output_examples.input: Ask how to batch prompts safely.
output_examples.output: The assistant recommends file based inputs, quoted data construction, separate output directories, retry logic, and token hygiene.

best_practices: Install the CLI through a verified package manager and inspect remote installers before use.
Keep RunComfy tokens out of prompts, logs, screenshots, and repositories.
Use explicit output directories so generated files are easy to review and clean up.
anti_patterns: Do not paste private credentials or sensitive source media into model prompts.
Do not pipe a remote installer into a shell without reviewing it first.
Do not use broad output paths that could mix generated files with project files.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code use the RunComfy CLI for authenticated model runs and automation.

faq.question: Does it install RunComfy automatically?
faq.answer: No. It explains installation options and then uses the CLI after the operator installs it.

faq.question: Does it send data outside my machine?
faq.answer: Yes. RunComfy model requests send prompts, inputs, and asset references to RunComfy services.

faq.question: Where are results saved?
faq.answer: Results download to the current directory by default, or to the directory selected with the output option.

faq.question: Can it run batch jobs?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes patterns for files of prompts, JSON output, request IDs, and retries.

faq.question: What security care is needed?
faq.answer: Protect tokens, review installers, trust only task-specific URLs, and monitor generated file downloads.
